essential_question: Why does hyp = leg√2 have to be true for every 45-45-90 triangle, straight from the Pythagorean theorem?
objective: Derive hyp = leg√2 from the Pythagorean theorem, justify why the 1:1:√2 ratio never changes, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on 45-45-90 triangles.

content.prompt: Without looking, draw a 45-45-90 triangle, label both legs and the hypotenuse, and write the rule hyp = leg√2 from memory. Then explain out loud, in your own words, why the two legs MUST be congruent.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 20A & 20B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 20A/20B. We check the tricky ones: rationalizing the denominator when the hypotenuse is given as a plain number with no radical in sight, simplifying a hypotenuse like (3√2)(√2) down to a whole number, and recognizing a square's diagonal as the hypotenuse of two hidden 45-45-90 triangles.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: hyp = leg√2 is true for every 45-45-90 triangle. Derive it from the Pythagorean theorem — start from leg² + leg² = hyp², factor, and solve for hyp. What forces the √2 to appear?
Claim 2: the ratio leg : leg : hypotenuse = 1 : 1 : √2 never changes, no matter how large or small the triangle is. Argue why scaling a 45-45-90 triangle up or down can't break this ratio.
Claim 3: to find a leg from a hypotenuse of 10, you can't stop at leg = 10/√2 — you have to rationalize. Argue why a radical is never left in a denominator, and show the rationalized form.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 21 — Special Right Triangles: 30-60-90 — where a different pair of angles hands you a different, but just as fixed, ratio of sides.

success_criteria.text: I can derive hyp = leg√2 from the Pythagorean theorem and explain why the 1:1:√2 ratio never changes
I can find the hypotenuse of a 45-45-90 triangle given a leg, simplifying radicals completely
I can find a leg of a 45-45-90 triangle given the hypotenuse, rationalizing the denominator without help

questions.prompt: A 45-45-90 triangle is also called an…
questions.explain: Its two 45° angles face two congruent legs, making it isosceles as well as right.

questions.prompt: Starting from leg² + leg² = hyp² and simplifying, the hypotenuse of a 45-45-90 triangle equals…
questions.explain: 2·leg² = hyp² → hyp = √(2·leg²) = leg√2 — the √2 appears because you're taking the square root of a doubled square.

questions.prompt: A 45-45-90 triangle has a leg of length 9. Find the length of the hypotenuse.
A 45-45-90 triangle has a leg of length 4√3. Find the hypotenuse, simplifying completely.
questions.explain: hyp = (4√3)(√2) = 4√6 — √6 has no perfect-square factor, so 4√6 is fully simplified.

questions.prompt: The figure shows a 45-45-90 triangle with legs of 4 units marked on the grid. Use a ruler to measure the hypotenuse directly on the page, then check: does your measurement match hyp = 4√2 ≈ 5.66?
questions.explain: Rubric: accept any ruler measurement within about half a unit of 5.66 — this confirms hyp = leg√2 is a real, checkable length, not just algebra.

questions.prompt: A 45-45-90 triangle has a hypotenuse of 20. Find the length of each leg, showing the rationalized form.
questions.explain: leg = 20/√2 = (20/√2)(√2/√2) = 20√2/2 = 10√2 — the denominator must be rationalized, never left as 20/√2.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 20 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
